alert.title: Health Alert
alert.message: Potential outbreak in Haramaya General Hospital. Risk level: UNKNOWN.
recommendations: Seek medical attention
Follow local health guidelines

risk_analysis.reason: The clinical triad of localized 'unusual insect bites' (likely representing a pruritic rash or vector exposure) combined with joint pain (arthralgia) is a classic presentation for alphaviral infections. Given the location in a banana plantation in Ethiopia—where Aedes and Anopheles mosquito vectors are prevalent—Chikungunya or O'nyong-nyong fever are the most probable matches. A cluster of 15 cases indicates an active transmission cycle requiring immediate vector control.

alert.title: Health Alert: Sudden Increase in Fever and Joint Pain in Arba Minch
alert.message: We have detected 15 cases of people working in Arba Minch banana plantations suffering from unusual insect bites and severe joint pain. These symptoms point to Chikungunya Fever, a disease carried by mosquitoes. Because many people in the same area are getting sick at once, we need to take action to stop it from spreading further to other workers and families.
recommendations: Go to the nearest health center immediately if you have a fever or painful joints.
Rest as much as possible and drink plenty of clean water if you feel unwell.
Tell your coworkers and neighbors to watch for these symptoms.
Do not take medicine without a doctor's advice, as some painkillers can be dangerous depending on the illness.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to stay safe is to avoid mosquito bites. Wear long-sleeved shirts and long trousers while working in the plantation. Use mosquito nets when sleeping and empty out any containers with standing water where mosquitoes like to lay their eggs.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 15 people in a small area got sick very quickly. Arba Minch's warm environment and the plantation conditions make it easy for mosquitoes to spread this disease fast if we don't protect ourselves now.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Illness Cases at Semera Port
alert.message: Three people at Semera Port have recently collapsed and experienced severe vomiting. This may be caused by the extreme heat in the area or a waterborne illness (Acute Watery Diarrhea). We are monitoring the situation closely to protect everyone at the port.
recommendations: Seek medical help immediately if you feel dizzy, extremely hot, or start vomiting.
Drink plenty of clean, safe water throughout the day, even if you are not thirsty.
If someone collapses, move them to a cool, shaded area and call for help right away.
Report any similar symptoms to port health officers or supervisors immediately.
alert.prevention_strategy: Stay cool and stay clean: Drink safe water, rest in the shade during the hottest parts of the day, and always wash your hands with soap and clean water before eating or touching your face.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was issued because the high temperatures in the Afar region can cause life-threatening heatstroke, and symptoms like vomiting can spread quickly if they are caused by a germ in the water or food.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Suspected Measles Outbreak in Jijiga Displacement Camp
alert.message: Five cases of suspected measles have been reported in a displacement camp in the Jijiga region. Measles is a very contagious disease that causes high fever and a red rash. Because the camp is crowded, the virus can spread very quickly from person to person. Health teams are working to confirm the cases and stop the spread.
recommendations: Take any child with a fever and a rash to the nearest health center immediately.
Keep sick children away from other people, especially babies and pregnant women.
Ensure all children in your family have received their measles vaccinations.
Cover your mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing.
Wash your hands often with soap and clean water.
alert.prevention_strategy: The most effective way to prevent measles is through vaccination. Two doses of the measles vaccine provide lifelong protection. In crowded areas, keeping living spaces clean and well-ventilated also helps reduce the risk of infection.
alert.why_urgent: This is urgent because measles is one of the most contagious diseases in the world. In a displacement camp where many people live close together, even 5 cases can quickly turn into a massive outbreak that puts many children's lives at risk.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Increase in Stomach Illnesses in Bahir Dar and Lake Tana Area
alert.message: Health officials have identified 8 cases of severe stomach illness near the Lake Tana area. Patients are suffering from intense nausea and abdominal pain. This cluster of cases suggests a waterborne infection, such as Cholera or Typhoid Fever, which can spread quickly through contaminated water and food.
recommendations: If you experience severe stomach pain, vomiting, or diarrhea, go to the nearest health center immediately.
Only drink water that has been boiled for at least one minute or treated with water purification tablets.
Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and clean water after using the latrine and before preparing or eating food.
Avoid eating raw vegetables or unpeeled fruits; ensure all food is cooked thoroughly and served hot.
Avoid swimming or bathing directly in Lake Tana until further notice.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to stay safe is the 'Boil, Wash, and Cook' method: Always boil your drinking water, wash your hands frequently with soap, and ensure all food is freshly cooked and handled with clean hands.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was issued because 8 people getting sick at the same time in one area is a warning sign of an outbreak. Because Lake Tana is a major water source, the risk of the illness spreading to more people is high unless we take action now.

While the symptoms could also align with vector-borne diseases like Dengue or Chikungunya given the mild tropical climate and proximity to Lake Hawassa, the rapid appearance of a rash-fever cluster in an urban center typically suggests a highly contagious viral exanthem. The risk is marked as Medium because while the case count is currently low compared to historical respiratory data, the potential for rapid transmission in a densely populated urban area like Hawassa is significant.

context_research.security_status: Generally stable and considered one of the safer urban centers in Ethiopia; however, it remains under the national security oversight due to broader regional tensions and historical ethnic friction in the Sidama region.

alert.title: Health Alert: Possible Measles Outbreak in Hawassa
alert.message: Health officials have identified 12 cases of suspected Measles (Rubeola) in Hawassa. Patients are reporting high fever and skin rashes. Because this many cases have appeared at once, there is a medium risk that the illness could spread further into the community if precautions are not taken immediately.
recommendations: Seek medical help immediately at the nearest health center if you or your child develop a high fever and a rash.
Keep children with symptoms at home and away from school or public places to prevent the virus from spreading.
Check your family's immunization records to ensure everyone has received their measles vaccinations.
Practice good hygiene by washing hands frequently with soap and water.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to prevent Measles is through vaccination. Ensure all children receive their two doses of the vaccine. Additionally, avoid crowded places and keep living areas well-ventilated to reduce the chance of the virus spreading through the air.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 12 cases in one area indicate the virus is actively moving through the community. Measles is highly contagious, and Hawassa’s population density makes it easy for the disease to spread quickly to many families if we don't act now.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Respiratory Outbreak in Mercato, Addis Ababa
alert.message: Health officials have identified a high-risk outbreak of respiratory illness in the Mercato area. So far, 45 people have reported symptoms including a dry cough and serious trouble breathing. This appears to be a fast-spreading viral pneumonia, similar to COVID-19 or a strong flu, and it requires immediate attention to keep the community safe.
recommendations: Wear a well-fitting face mask at all times when in public areas or markets.
If you have a dry cough or find it hard to breathe, go to the nearest health center immediately.
Avoid crowded places and keep at least two arm-lengths of distance from others.
Wash your hands with soap and water frequently, especially after touching surfaces in public.
Stay home if you feel unwell or have any symptoms of a cold or flu.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to stay safe is to stop germs from moving between people. Cover your mouth and nose with a mask, keep your hands clean, and avoid close contact with anyone who is coughing or sneezing.
alert.why_urgent: This is urgent because 45 cases have appeared very quickly in a short time. Mercato is one of the most crowded areas in the city, which means the sickness can spread to hundreds of people very fast if we do not act now.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Respiratory Illness Outbreak in Mercato
alert.message: A high-risk health alert has been issued for the Mercato area of Addis Ababa. In the last 48 hours, 45 people have fallen ill with severe respiratory symptoms, including dry coughs and difficulty breathing. Health experts believe this is a highly contagious viral pneumonia, similar to COVID-19 or a strong flu variant, that is spreading quickly through the community.
recommendations: Seek medical attention immediately if you have a persistent dry cough or trouble breathing.
Wear a face mask at all times when in public, especially in crowded market areas.
Avoid the Mercato area for the next few days unless it is absolutely necessary.
Wash your hands with soap and water frequently or use hand sanitizer.
If you feel sick, stay home and keep away from family members to stop the spread.
alert.prevention_strategy: To stay safe, keep a distance of at least two meters from others, wear a mask over your nose and mouth, and avoid touching your face. Keeping your hands clean and staying away from crowded spots are the best ways to protect yourself and your family.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 45 people got sick in a very short time in one of the most crowded areas of the city. Because so many people visit Mercato every day, there is a high risk that this illness could spread across all of Addis Ababa very quickly if we do not act now.
